analogy: Think of "Electric Charges and Fields" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter One ELECTRIC CHARGES AND FIELDS 1.1 INTRODUCTION All of us have the experience of seeing a spark or hearing a crackle when we take off our synthetic clothes or sweater, particularly in dry weather. Have you ever tried to find any explanation for this... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: ELECTRIC CHARGES, AND FIELDS, 1.1 INTRODUCTION, 1.2 ELECTRIC CHARGE. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Electric Charges and Fields is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that chapter One ELECTRIC CHARGES AND FIELDS 1.1 INTRODUCTION All of us have the experience of seeing a spark or hearing a crackle when we take off our synthetic clothes or sweater, particularly in dry weather.

The text explains that have you ever tried to find any explanation for this phenomenon?

The text explains that another common example of electric discharge is the lightning that we see in the sky during thunderstorms.

The text explains that we also experience a sensation of an electric shock either while opening the door of a car or holding the iron bar of a bus after sliding from our seat.

The text explains that the reason for these experiences is discharge of electric charges through our body, which were accumulated due to rubbing of insulating surfaces.

The text explains that you might have also heard that this is due to generation of static electricity.

The text explains that this is precisely the topic we are going to discuss in this and the next chapter.

The text explains that static means anything that does not move or change with time.

The text explains that electrostatics deals with the study of forces, fields and potentials arising from static charges.

The text explains that 1.2 ELECTRIC CHARGE Historically the credit of discovery of the fact that amber rubbed with wool or silk cloth attracts light objects goes to Thales of Miletus, Greece, around 600 BC.

The text explains that the name electricity is coined from the Greek word

The text explains that many such pairs of materials were known which on rubbing could attract light objects like straw, pith balls and bits of papers.

The text explains that it was observed that if two glass rods rubbed with wool or silk cloth are brought close to each other, they repel each other [Fig.

The text explains that the two strands of wool or two pieces of silk cloth, with which the rods were rubbed, also repel each other.

The text explains that however, the glass rod and wool attracted each other.

The text explains that similarly, two plastic rods rubbed with cat’s fur repelled each other [Fig.

The text explains that no new charge is created in the process of rubbing.

The text explains that metals, human and animal bodies and earth are conductors.

The text explains that you will learn why this happens in the next chapter.

The text explains that if a system contains two point charges q1 and q2, the total charge of the system is obtained simply by adding algebraically q1 and q2 , i.e., charges add up like real numbers or they are scalars like the mass of a body.

The text explains that if a system contains n charges q1, q2, q3, …, qn, then the total charge of the system is q1 + q2 + q3 + … + qn .

The text explains that charge has magnitude but no direction, similar to mass.

The text explains that proper signs have to be used while adding the charges in a system.

The text explains that for example, the total charge of a system containing five charges +1, +2, –3, +4 and –5, in some arbitrary unit, is (+1) + (+2) + (–3) + (+4) + (–5) = –1 in the same unit.

The text explains that within an isolated system consisting of many charged bodies, due to interactions among the bodies, charges may get redistributed but it is found that the total charge of the isolated system is always conserved.

The text explains that it is not possible to create or destroy net charge carried by any isolated system although the charge carrying particles may be created or destroyed FIGURE 1.2 Electroscopes: (a) The gold leaf electroscope, (b) Schematics of a simple electroscope.

The text explains that in the International System (SI) of Units, a unit of charge is called a coulomb and is denoted by the symbol C.

The text explains that a coulomb is defined in terms the unit of the electric current which you are going to learn in a subsequent chapter.

The text explains that in terms of this definition, one coulomb is the charge flowing through a wire in 1 s if the current is 1 A (ampere), (see Chapter 1 of Class XI, Physics Textbook , Part I).

The text explains that in this system, the value of the basic unit of charge is e = 1.602192 × 10–19 C Thus, there are about 6 × 1018 electrons in a charge of –1C.

The text explains that coulomb measured the force between two point charges and found that it varied inversely as the square of the distance between the charges and was directly proportional to the product of the magnitude of the two charges and

The text explains that repeating this process, we can get charges q/2, q/4, etc.

The text explains that the direction of a vector is specified by a unit vector along the vector.

The text explains that to denote the direction from 1 to 2 (or from 2 to 1), we define the unit vectors: ɵ 21 21 21 = r r r , ɵ ɵ ɵ = = − 12 12 21 12 12 , r r r r r Coulomb’s force law between two point charges q1 and q2 located at r1 and r2, respectively is then expressed as ɵ ε = π 1 2 21 21 2 21 1 4 o q q r F r (1.3) Some remarks on Eq.

The text explains that we shall consider electrostatics in matter in the next chapter.

The text explains that consider a system of n stationary charges q1, q2, q3, ..., qn in vacuum.

The text explains that coulomb’s law is not enough to answer this question.

The text explains that to better understand the concept, consider a system of three charges q1, q2 and q3, as shown in Fig.

The text explains that thus, F12 = 1 4 0 1 2 12 2 12 π ε q q r ˆr FIGURE 1.5 A system of (a) three charges (b) multiple charges.

The text explains that thus the total force F1 on q1 due to the two charges q2 and q3 is given as F F F r r 1 12 13 0 1 2 12 2 12 0 1 3 13 2 13 1 4 1 4 = + = + π π ε ε q q r q q r ˆ ˆ (1.4) The above calculation of force can be generalised to a system of charges more than three, as shown in Fig.

The text explains that the principle of superposition says that in a system of charges q1, q2, ..., qn, the force on q1 due to q2 is the same as given by Coulomb’s law, i.e., it is unaffected by the presence of the other charges q3, q4, ..., qn.

The text explains that suppose that the resultant force was non-zero but in some direction.

The text explains that consider what would happen if the system was rotated through 60° about O.

The text explains that 14 Physics EXAMPLE 1.6 Similarly the total force on charge –q at C is F3 = 3 F ˆn , where ˆn is the unit vector along the direction bisecting the ÐBCA.

The text explains that we may ask the question: If charge q is removed, then what is left in the surrounding?

The text explains that in order to answer such questions, the early scientists introduced the concept of field.

The text explains that thus, the electric field due to a charge Q at a point in space may be defined as the force that a unit positive charge would experience if placed * An alternate unit V/m will be introduced in the next chapter.

The text explains that the charge Q, which is producing the electric field, is called a source charge and the charge q, which tests the effect of a source charge, is called a test charge.

The text explains that note that the source charge Q must remain at its original location.

The text explains that 1.19 A point charge causes an electric flux of –1.0 × 103 Nm2/C to pass through a spherical Gaussian surface of 10.0 cm radius centred on the charge.

The text explains that (a) If the radius of the Gaussian surface were doubled, how much flux would pass through the surface?

The text explains that 1.20 A conducting sphere of radius 10 cm has an unknown charge.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Physics, Chapter 1: Electric Charges and Fields

prelims_traps: Do not treat ELECTRIC CHARGES and AND FIELDS as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Electric Charges and Fields; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Electric Charges and Fields through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use ELECTRIC CHARGES and AND FIELDS to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
